question.text: When do arbitrage opportunities between spot and futures markets cease to exist?

answer.text: When sufficient arbitrageurs exploit the mispricing, driving prices back to equilibrium where the futures price equals its fair value
When the exchange imposes position limits on arbitrage traders
When market volatility reaches zero and all trading stops
When the central bank intervenes to fix both spot and futures prices

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Arbitrage activity is self-correcting. When traders identify mispricing (futures above or below fair value), they simultaneously trade in spot and futures markets. This buying/selling pressure moves prices back toward equilibrium. The opportunity disappears once prices are aligned. **The Trap:** Exchanges don't stop arbitrage through position limits — they facilitate it. Central banks don't fix futures prices. Arbitrage ceases when price equilibrium is restored, not when trading stops. **Cross-Exam Context:** All three series (I, IV, VIII) describe this self-correcting mechanism of arbitrage in their respective markets. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Arbitrage is the invisible hand that keeps spot and futures prices aligned — without it, markets would have persistent mispricings.
